title: Improve ad-hoc UI verification scripts with field assertions and stronger wait/debug support
description:
## Description
Improve the small ad-hoc UI verification toolkit based on the feedback from recent verifier usage.

## Why
The toolkit is already useful, especially `scripts/capture_bwb_screen.py`, but recent verifier sessions showed a few focused improvements would make it faster and more reliable to use in practice without turning it into a framework.

## What should be done
### 1. Field-specific state assertions in the wrapper
Improve `scripts/verify_bwb_state_flow.py` so representative flows can verify explicit fields instead of only shallow whole-object changed/unchanged checks.

Target implementation direction:
- add an explicit small `expect_fields` / equivalent mapping per representative flow
- support simple expectations such as:
  - field changed
  - field unchanged
  - optional exact expected value when practical

Keep this small and flow-specific; do not build a generic assertion language.

### 2. One stronger ephemeral/readiness helper in the PTY script
Improve `scripts/capture_bwb_screen.py` by adding a small wait primitive for transient UI messages.

Chosen scope:
- add `wait-for-text-once:<TEXT>[:timeout-ms]`
- semantics: pass once the text has appeared at least once during the wait window, even if it later disappears

This directly helps toast/ephemeral feedback checks without framework creep.

### 3. Better failure/debug evidence
Improve failure output so when a step fails it is easier to understand why.

Chosen scope:
- increase observed failure excerpt usefulness
- automatically include the latest checkpoint (if any) in failure output
- include a small recent raw-output tail on failure when available

### 4. Keep the wrapper thin
Do not turn `verify_bwb_state_flow.py` into a broad framework. It should remain a small convenience layer around:
- running the PTY script
- taking before/after `bd show --json` snapshots
- checking a few explicit fields

## Constraints
- Keep the toolkit small and ad-hoc oriented.
- No branching DSL or generalized framework behavior.
- Preserve the current usefulness of `capture_bwb_screen.py` as the main tool.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Wrapper supports explicit field-targeted state verification for representative mutation checks.
- [ ] PTY helper supports `wait-for-text-once` for transient/ephemeral-message verification.
- [ ] Failure output includes more useful debugging evidence than before (improved excerpt plus latest checkpoint/raw-tail context where available).
- [ ] A representative mutation-save flow passes with field assertions.
- [ ] One intentional failing probe demonstrates the improved failure output.
- [ ] The implementation stays small and script/tooling oriented.
- [ ] Real-app verification confirms the improved scripts are still practical for ad-hoc UI testing.
close_reason: Implemented field-targeted wrapper assertions, wait-for-text-once helper, and richer failure evidence; validated with passing mutation-save flow and intentional failing probes.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Overall verdict: Task is well-scoped and grounded in real feedback. The anti-framework guardrail is good. Three findings follow as separate comments.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: AC#1 is too vague to verify. What: 'explicit field-targeted state verification for representative mutation checks' doesn't specify which fields, what assertion syntax, or what happens on mismatch. The implementer has to invent the API. Why: The current shallow_diff in verify_bwb_state_flow.py (line 90) only reports changed keys — extending it to per-field assertions is straightforward, but the AC doesn't constrain HOW (CLI flags? flow-spec additions? inline dict?). This will cause review churn. Suggested action: Replace AC#1 with something like 'FlowSpec gains an optional expect_fields dict (e.g. {"status": "closed"}) checked against the after snapshot; mismatches appear in the JSON output with before/after values. mutation-save flow uses it for at least status and closed_at.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: AC#2 readiness improvement is underspecified. What: 'at least one small but meaningful readiness/ephemeral-message improvement' — three different directions are listed (modal title, text-appears-once, settled-screen) with no guidance on which one. Why: These are very different in complexity. A settled-screen helper (detecting when screen stops changing) is meaningfully harder and more fragile than a wait-for-text-once helper. An implementer choosing settled-screen could easily blow the 'keep it small' constraint. Suggested action: Pick ONE. Recommendation: add a wait-for-text-once step type (wait until text appears, then return immediately without requiring it to persist). It's the simplest, directly addresses ephemeral flash messages, and fits the existing step parser with minimal change.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: AC#3 failure evidence is already partially there — scope it tighter. What: capture_bwb_screen.py already emits observed_excerpt (20 lines) and checkpoint screens on failure. The description says 'larger or more useful observed excerpts' and 'recent raw-output tail' but doesn't say what's actually missing today. Why: Without a concrete gap statement, the implementer may add noise (e.g. dumping raw bytes by default) that makes output harder to read, not easier. Suggested action: Rewrite AC#3 to: 'On failure, the JSON output includes the last checkpoint screen (if any) and the raw-output tail (last 2KB) automatically, without requiring --dump-raw. The failure object includes observed_excerpt expanded to 30 lines.' This is concrete and small.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Simplification opportunity: items 1-3 could be two tasks, not one. What: Field assertions (wrapper change) and readiness/failure-evidence (PTY helper changes) touch different files with no dependency. Why: Bundling them means partial completion is hard to land, and review scope is wider. Suggested action: Consider splitting into two tasks — one for wrapper field assertions, one for PTY readiness+failure improvements. But this is optional; the current scope is small enough (~100 lines of change total) that a single task is defensible if the ACs are tightened per the comments above.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: AC#5 'Real-app verification confirms the improved scripts are still practical' is not testable as written. What: No definition of what 'practical' means or what constitutes real-app verification. Why: This will either be rubber-stamped or argued about. Suggested action: Replace with 'The mutation-save flow passes with the new field assertions against a fixture beads project, and the improved failure output is demonstrated by intentionally failing one step.'

title: Detail quick actions advertise wrong add-comment key
description:
## Description
Align the detail quick-actions rail with the actual add-comment binding so the advertised hotkey opens the comment modal.

## Instructions
1. Treat the resolved shell keybinding as the source of truth for the add-comment action.
2. Update the detail quick-actions rail and any focused tests or goldens so the advertised key matches the real binding, or change the binding if that is the intended product behavior.
3. Verify on the seeded fixture that the advertised key opens the comment modal from detail view after the mutation catalog modal bug is fixed.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/ui/details/details.go`
- `internal/ui/details/details_test.go`
- `internal/ui/details/testdata/*.golden`
- `internal/config/keybindings.go` (only if the canonical fix is to change the binding)
- `internal/app/model_test.go`
acceptance_criteria: In detail view, the advertised add-comment hotkey opens the comment modal on the seeded fixture. The detail quick-actions and help text match the actual add-comment binding, with no misleading c or a mismatch.
close_reason: fixed: quick action labels now follow resolved keybindings and built-binary verification passes

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Triage note: keep separate from beads-workbench-9lze. The primary defect here is a detail-view hotkey/help mismatch ( is advertised for add-comment but routes elsewhere, while  currently opens the comment modal). The mutation-catalog toast is only a secondary symptom of the wrong route.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Correction to prior triage note: keep this separate from beads-workbench-9lze. Primary defect: the detail-view add-comment hotkey/help is mismatched. The UI advertises "c Add comment", but "c" routes elsewhere while "a" currently opens the comment modal. The mutation-catalog toast is only a secondary symptom of the wrong route.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Reproduced on 2026-04-22 against the current workspace build. In detail view, the quick-actions rail still renders "c Add comment" while the actual shell binding remains "a" (`ShellActionCommentIssue`). PTY verification with scripts/capture_bwb_screen.py confirmed pressing a opens the "Comment on <issue>" modal, so the mismatch is still present and this bug remains relevant.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Fixed on 2026-04-22. Detail metadata quick actions now render from resolved shell keybindings instead of a hard-coded add-comment label, and the app initializes detail mode with the resolved key map. Verified with focused tests and a rebuilt-binary PTY run: the detail rail shows "a Add comment" on the seeded fixture and pressing a opens the comment modal.

title: Runtime verification doc still documents legacy flow instead of new step/wrapper toolkit
description: docs/RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md still shows only legacy --steps delay:key recipes and legacy failure text like 'invalid step ... expected delay:key'. It does not document the new repeatable --step wait-for-text / wait-for-no-text workflow or the verify_bwb_state_flow wrapper usage. Expected: the runtime verification runbook documents the new condition-based step syntax and the before/after wrapper path that this epic added, not only the pre-existing blind-delay commands.
acceptance_criteria: RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md includes concise recipes using the current --step wait-based helper and documents the wrapper or equivalent current persisted-state workflow without relying solely on legacy --steps syntax.
close_reason: Updated runtime verification runbook to document wait-based --step toolkit and verify_bwb_state_flow wrapper recipes instead of legacy-only delay:key flow.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: Clear and well-scoped. The epic's own success criteria say 'docs/RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md documents the workflow concisely with practical recipes' — so a doc that still shows only legacy syntax means the epic's doc task (.3) was closed prematurely. Verdict: EPIC BLOCKER — this is a gap in the epic's own deliverable, not optional polish. Suggested action: fix this as part of closing the epic, not as follow-up.

title: Align search layout and goldens with detail content/metadata flow
description:
## Description
Align search mode with the dedicated detail experience so search uses the same content and metadata components, while keeping a search-focused left rail for query and results. This should replace the current compact preview-only rendering and bring the resulting search goldens back into sync with the intended UI.

## Instructions
1. Add a wide search layout with three panes: a left search rail for query and results, a center content pane reused from detail rendering, and a right metadata pane reused from detail rendering.
2. Add a narrow/small-screen fallback layout that stays usable while still reusing the same shared content and metadata rendering pieces.
3. Preserve the current search browsing behavior where moving the result selection immediately updates the content and metadata panes for the newly selected issue without requiring Enter.
4. Reuse the very same content and metadata rendering components/helpers used by dedicated detail mode so future changes to those panes are visible in both screens.
5. Keep the search rail wider than the detail dependencies rail in the wide layout so search remains comfortable for triage, but trim it slightly if needed to give shared content and metadata panes enough space.
6. Preserve the search-specific differences from dedicated detail mode: the left pane is search/query/results instead of dependencies, and no dependency browser is shown in search.
7. Update focused search and mode tests plus committed goldens to reflect both the wide and narrow intended behavior.
8. Verify the focused search/detail packages and the full repository test suite.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/ui/search/search.go`
- `internal/ui/search/search_test.go`
- `internal/ui/search/testdata/*.golden`
- `internal/mode/search/model.go`
- `internal/mode/search/model_test.go`
- `internal/app/model.go` (if search needs loaded detail state from shell)
- `internal/ui/details/details.go` (if shared content/metadata helpers need extraction or direct reuse)
- `internal/ui/details/metadata.go` (if shared metadata rendering needs adjustment)
acceptance_criteria: Wide search layout renders Search | Content | Metadata using the same shared content and metadata renderers as dedicated detail mode, narrow search layout falls back to a smaller responsive layout while keeping shared rendering, changing the selected search result updates content and metadata without Enter, and `go test ./internal/ui/search ./internal/mode/search ./internal/ui/details ./internal/mode/details` plus `go test ./...` pass.
close_reason: Implemented and verified: search now reuses shared detail content/metadata panes with wide+narrow layouts, live preview, and full test coverage.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: Clearly unrelated to the verification toolkit epic. The golden test diffs (Status before Priority) suggest a rendering order change was made somewhere without regenerating goldens. Acceptance criteria are fine. Verdict: NOT AN EPIC BLOCKER for ay5j. Fix independently. Suggested action: regenerate goldens with 'go test ./internal/ui/search -update' (or equivalent) after confirming the new order is intentional.

FAIL	github.com/hk9890/beads-workbench/internal/ui/search	0.021s
FAIL. Both issues describe the same three failing search golden cases and will be fixed by the same rendering-order decision or golden regeneration.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Architectural finding: True component reuse is structurally feasible but requires a small refactor first. What: ui/details exposes only Render(State) publicly; the genuinely reusable units (renderContentPaneLines, renderMetadataPaneLines, renderMetadataRail, splitThreePaneWidths, sliceWithOffset, MaxScrollOffsets per-pane) are package-private. uidetails.Render with Compact:true does not produce the same boxed Content/Metadata panes detail mode shows. Why: 'reuse the same renderer' as written in the issue is misleading — search would either (a) duplicate the FormSection wrapping logic or (b) we extract small public helpers like RenderContentPane(detail, width, height, focused, scrollOffset) string and RenderMetadataPane(detail, width, height, focused, scrollOffset, selectedField) string. Suggested action: before any UI work, add an acceptance criterion that ui/details exposes per-pane render helpers that BOTH detail mode and search mode call, and rewrite renderThreePane to consume those same helpers so divergence is impossible by construction.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Shell architecture finding: detail-on-selection already works, but search must NOT own its own detail loader. What: app/model.go already runs ensureDetailForCurrentSelectionCmd() on every mode.SelectionChangedMsg from board OR search (line 387-395), and m.detail is shell-owned. Search currently throws this loaded detail away and renders only its in-memory IssueSummary via uidetails.Render(Compact:true). Why: this means the data plumbing for 'arrow keys update content+metadata' is already in place — search just isn't reading from it. The cleanest implementation is for search.View to receive m.detail (the loaded IssueDetail) from the shell and pass it to the shared content/metadata helpers. Suggested action: change search.Render's State to accept a fully loaded domain.IssueDetail (provided by the shell from m.detail.Detail when m.detail.SelectionID == search's selected ID); do NOT add a second detail loader inside the search mode.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Risk finding: stale-detail flicker during search typing. What: when the user types a new query, results change rapidly and the previously-loaded m.detail.Detail will not match the new top selection until the next loadDetailCmd resolves. If search renders the heavy content/metadata panes from m.detail, they will briefly show the previous issue's content alongside the new highlighted row in the rail. Why: today's compact preview hides this because it falls back to rendering from the in-memory IssueSummary (search.go lines 182-193). Suggested action: define explicit behavior — when SelectionID != m.detail.Detail.Summary.ID (or m.detail.Loading), render content/metadata panes from the IssueSummary stub plus a 'Loading…' affordance, mirroring the existing loading.View path. Add this as an acceptance criterion and a golden case.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Navigation finding: focus-cycle semantics in search mode have a real conflict with the rail being a query+results stack. What: detail mode treats Left/Right as a flat 3-pane traversal (Dependencies <-> Content <-> Metadata). The proposal says search should mirror this with rail<->Content<->Metadata, but the search 'rail' is itself two stacked sub-panes (Query and Results) and today distinguishes FocusQuery from FocusResults so up/down means 'edit query' vs 'move selection'. Why: collapsing both into a single 'rail' focus state breaks query editing; keeping them separate makes search a 4-state focus (Query, Results, Content, Metadata) which does NOT mirror detail mode and complicates 'left from Content goes to ???'. Suggested action: pick one explicitly in the issue — either (a) Left from Content -> Results, and Query is reachable only via the dedicated focus-query keybinding (recommended, preserves typing UX), or (b) Left from Content -> Results, Left from Results -> Query. Document the chosen rule in acceptance criteria with a key-by-key walkthrough.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Width finding: three full-height boxed panes will not fit at the w80 narrow golden. What: detail mode's splitThreePaneWidths reserves metadataRailWidth=34, leftRailMin=24, content min=20, plus 2x detailColumnGap=4 -> minimum ~82 chars before truncation kicks in hard. Search currently has a separate w80 narrow golden (search_results_narrow_w80.golden) that today fits because preview is a single right pane. Why: forcing three boxed panes at 80 cols will produce illegible output and probably break/regenerate the narrow golden in an ugly way. Suggested action: define the responsive rule explicitly — at widths below ~110, fall back to the current 2-pane layout (rail + combined preview) or hide the metadata pane. Add this as an acceptance criterion and provide both narrow and wide goldens.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Scope finding: original bug (golden mismatch from metadata indentation/order change) is being inflated into a redesign. What: the failing goldens in the description show only two regressions — Status/Priority order swap and a leading two-space indent in the metadata block. These come from a metadata renderer change in detail mode bleeding into search's compact preview. The redesign to three shared panes is a separate, larger user-driven scope (captured in epic 2shr comment). Why: bundling them risks the redesign blocking on cosmetic golden churn and vice versa. Suggested action: split into two issues — keep aqps narrowly as 'regenerate search goldens after metadata renderer change' (small, can land today), and create a new task 'Search mode three-pane redesign with shared content/metadata components' that owns the architectural work and the new acceptance criteria. The redesign should depend on the helper-extraction refactor recommended in earlier comment, not on this golden fix.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Acceptance criteria gaps. What: the current AC says 'left/right pane navigation matches detail-mode semantics' and 'changing selected search result automatically refreshes content and metadata without Enter' but does not specify: (1) the responsive fallback below ~110 cols, (2) the loading/stale-detail rendering rule when m.detail.SelectionID lags the search highlight, (3) the exact 4-state vs 3-state focus model for search (Query/Results/Content/Metadata), (4) whether the existing 'Preview' box title is replaced by 'Content'/'Metadata' titles to match detail mode (goldens will need this), (5) which detail-mode keys carry over (page-up/down, home/end on Content pane?), (6) whether dependencies are silently omitted or whether a one-line summary 'Blocked by: N · Blocks: N · Related: N' is shown on the rail. Why: without these spelled out, the implementer will guess and the verifier can't deterministically pass/fail. Suggested action: add each item above as an explicit acceptance bullet before starting implementation.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Simpler alternative worth considering. What: rather than bringing the full three-pane detail layout into search, achieve 95% of the user's intent by (a) extracting RenderContentPane and RenderMetadataPane public helpers, (b) replacing search's current single right-side 'Preview' box with two stacked boxes on the right ('Content' on top, 'Metadata' below) both rendered via those shared helpers, (c) keeping today's left rail untouched (Search + Results), (d) keeping today's left/right and cycle key behavior with the third focus state being 'Metadata' added at the end. Why: this avoids the responsive layout problem (still 2 columns), preserves the existing query+results focus split, still gives true component reuse of content+metadata, and is a much smaller diff than re-doing the whole layout as 3 columns. Suggested action: ask the user explicitly whether they need 3 horizontal columns or whether a 2-column layout with stacked Content+Metadata on the right satisfies the 'shared rendering' goal. If 3 columns is non-negotiable, proceed with the full redesign and the responsive fallback above.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Maintenance risk: detail mode owns dialogs/intents (status, priority, dependency Enter-to-open) on its metadata and dependencies panes. What: details.Model.HandleKey produces OpenRelatedIssueIntent and pendingOpenStatus/PriorityDialog when those panes are focused. If search's Metadata pane is genuinely 'the same' component, users will reasonably expect Enter on Status to open the status dialog from search too. Why: either we wire those intents through the search mode (extra complexity and tests) or we silently disable them in search (surprises users — 'why does Enter work in detail but not search?'). Suggested action: pick one explicitly. Recommended: enable the same Enter-on-metadata-field behavior in search by routing through the existing m.detail intents at the shell level (m.detail.SelectionID is already kept in sync). Add this as an acceptance criterion or explicitly defer with 'metadata fields are read-only in search mode' in the AC.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Responsive fallback is now explicitly in scope for search. Detail-mode dependency/browser live-preview semantics are tracked separately in beads-workbench-2shr.2 so aqps stays search-focused.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: User approved the search direction: wide layout plus small-width fallback, shared detail content/metadata renderers, and reviewer requested before implementation.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verdict on approved direction: SOUND but order-of-operations matters. What: the approved 3-pane wide layout + small-width fallback + shared content/metadata renderers is the right shape, and earlier comments already enumerated the helper extraction needed. Why: without doing the helper extraction FIRST as its own commit, the implementer will inevitably either (a) duplicate FormSection wrapping or (b) inline-copy renderContentPaneLines/renderMetadataPaneLines into search.go and the two surfaces will drift again within a release. Suggested action: add an explicit acceptance criterion 'ui/details exposes RenderContentPane(detail, width, height, focused, scrollOffset) string and RenderMetadataPane(detail, width, height, focused, scrollOffset, selectedField) string, and detail mode's renderThreePane is rewritten to consume those same exported helpers' as the FIRST item, before any search-layout work.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Coupling finding with 2shr.2: aqps and 2shr.2 share the EXACT SAME prerequisite (exported per-pane render helpers) and the SAME stale-detail loading rule. What: both issues need (a) RenderContentPane/RenderMetadataPane public helpers and (b) a deterministic 'SelectionID does not match m.detail.Detail.Summary.ID -> render from IssueSummary stub with loading affordance' rule. Why: if aqps lands first and defines this rule one way, 2shr.2 inherits it for free; if they're built independently the loading affordances will diverge between search and detail-dependency live preview. Suggested action: keep the issues split (different layouts, different focus models) but mark 2shr.2 as 'blocked-by aqps' and call out in aqps that its helper extraction and stale-detail rule MUST be reusable by detail-mode dependency browsing.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Shell finding: do NOT add a search-owned detail loader. What: app/model.go:387-395 already runs ensureDetailForCurrentSelectionCmd on every SelectionChangedMsg from search; m.detail.Detail is the canonical loaded detail. The mode/search/model.go View() currently throws this away and only renders from in-memory IssueSummary via uidetails.Render(Compact:true). Why: any second loader would race the shell's loader and create double-fetches. Suggested action: change uisearch.State to accept a LoadedDetail domain.IssueDetail field; the shell (app.Model.renderBody) constructs the State and passes m.detail.Detail when m.detail.SelectionID == search's selected ID and m.detail.Loading == false, otherwise passes the IssueSummary stub. Add this as an explicit acceptance criterion: 'mode/search must not call gateway.ShowIssue; preview pane data is supplied by the shell.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Stale-preview rule (acceptance criterion missing): What: when m.detail.SelectionID != m.detail.Detail.Summary.ID OR m.detail.Loading is true, the search Content+Metadata panes must render a deterministic placeholder, not stale prior-issue data. Why: today's compact preview hides this by falling back to the IssueSummary, but full Content+Metadata panes will visibly flash the previous issue's description/comments when the user arrows down a row faster than gateway.ShowIssue resolves. Suggested action: add AC bullet 'while highlighted result has not been loaded yet, Content pane shows title/ID/status from IssueSummary plus a single Loading… line; Metadata pane shows only the Core group from IssueSummary (Type/Status/Priority); both panes show the same loading affordance the dedicated detail loading.View uses.' Add a golden case for this state.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Responsive fallback rule (acceptance criterion still under-specified). What: detail mode's splitThreePaneWidths reserves metadataRailWidth=34 + leftRailMin=24 + minContent=20 + 2x detailColumnGap=4 = ~82 chars before truncation kicks in hard, and search rail wants to be WIDER than detail's left rail. The current narrow golden is at w80. Why: 'small/narrow fallback layout' is too vague — implementer + verifier need a number. Suggested action: pick an explicit threshold and document it: e.g., 'at width >= 110, render Search rail | Content | Metadata; at width < 110, render Search rail | (Content stacked over Metadata) in a single right column; below ~70 fall back to results-only'. Goldens should exist for at minimum w80 (narrow), w110 (boundary), w140 (wide). Add the breakpoint number to AC, not just 'small fallback'.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Focus-model gap (still): What: with FocusQuery + FocusResults + new Content + new Metadata, search has 4 focus states while detail has 3. Approved direction did not pick a key-by-key rule. Why: ambiguous Left-from-Content behavior breaks both implementer and tests. Suggested action (recommended): treat the search rail as a single 'rail' for Left/Right traversal — Left from Content -> Results (NOT Query); Query is reachable only via the dedicated focus-query keybinding (config.SearchActionFocusQuery, today '/'). Up/Down inside Results is the live-preview driver; typing while Query is focused still triggers triggerSearch. Document this exact rule as an AC bullet and add a focused mode test asserting the focus transitions for {Left, Right, Tab, Shift-Tab, /}.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Metadata interactivity decision still missing. What: detail mode's metadata pane reacts to Enter on Status/Priority by emitting pendingOpenStatusDialog/pendingOpenPriorityDialog, which the shell turns into modals (app/model.go:425-453). If search's Metadata pane is the same component, users will reasonably expect those keys to work. Why: silently disabling them in search will surprise users; enabling them requires routing the same intents from search through the shell. Suggested action: pick one explicitly. Recommended: enable Enter-on-Status/Priority in search by promoting ConsumeOpenStatusDialogIntent/ConsumeOpenPriorityDialogIntent to a shared intent path that the shell checks regardless of active mode (search OR detail). Add the chosen rule to AC and add a search-mode test that Enter on metadata Status with FocusMetadata produces a status modal request.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Tests/goldens essential before merge. What: (1) wide layout golden Search|Content|Metadata at w140 with selection loaded; (2) wide golden when SelectionID != Detail.Summary.ID (loading affordance); (3) narrow fallback golden at w80; (4) boundary golden at the chosen breakpoint (e.g., w110); (5) empty-results wide golden (no Content/Metadata, just placeholders); (6) focused mode test asserting m.search does not call gateway.ShowIssue when arrowing through results; (7) focused app/model_test asserting SelectionChangedMsg from search still triggers ensureDetailForCurrentSelectionCmd; (8) focused detail render tests must keep passing UNCHANGED after helper extraction (proves the refactor was a no-op for detail mode). Suggested action: add this test list as an AC checklist so the implementer cannot skip the loading/breakpoint cases.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Implementation decisions locked for execution: extract shared detail RenderContentPane/RenderMetadataPane helpers first; search uses shell-owned loaded detail state (no search-owned ShowIssue loader); stale preview rule is shared with detail live preview; width >= 110 uses Search | Content | Metadata and width < 110 uses a narrow fallback; left from Content returns to Results while query remains reachable via the focus-query binding; metadata enter actions should remain interactive in search using the same intent path as detail mode.

title: Create and update modals fail when mutation catalogs load
description: Exploratory runtime run against the seeded fixture found that create and update actions fail before their modals open. Repro: build /tmp/bwb, seed embedded fixture, wait for Selected: bwf-2, then press c or u from the board. Actual: a toast appears: 'Failed to load mutation catalogs: label catalog ... failed to decode command JSON output ...' and no modal opens. Expected: Create Issue and Update Issue modals open successfully. Related existing bug: beads-workbench-9lze tracks a similar mutation-catalog failure reported for comment flow; create/update are still concretely reproducible in the current app.
acceptance_criteria: On the seeded fixture, board-level create and update actions open their modals without a mutation-catalog decode toast.
close_reason: Duplicate of beads-workbench-9lze after triage: same mutation catalog JSON decode failure across different modal entry points.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: Real product bug, clearly written, good repro steps. Note the link to beads-workbench-9lze — check if that's a dupe or if 9lze covers only comment flow while this covers create/update. Verdict: NOT AN EPIC BLOCKER for ay5j. This is a product bug in modal/catalog loading, not in the verification toolkit. The toolkit correctly surfaced it, which is actually evidence the toolkit works. Track separately.

title: Wrapper mutation-save preset exits before persisted change is provable
description:
verify_bwb_state_flow mutation-save can report changed=false even when the UI flow succeeded, because the preset exits too quickly after confirmation. Repro on a seeded open issue: wrapper returns unchanged even though a direct capture plus a short additional wait shows the issue becomes closed. Expected: mutation-save should wait for a durable post-submit success signal before comparing before/after state.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Mutation-save waits for a concrete settled post-submit signal before capturing after-state.
- [ ] The settlement signal is observable in the UI or flow (for example modal disappearance, toast, return to board/detail state, or equivalent deterministic condition), not an arbitrary blind sleep.
- [ ] The seeded open-issue close flow reports changed=true without caller-added sleeps.
acceptance_criteria: Mutation-save waits for a settled post-submit state and reports changed=true on the seeded open-issue close flow without caller-added sleeps.
close_reason: Mutation-save now waits for deterministic post-submit settlement signals before after-state capture; seeded close flow reports changed=true without extra sleeps.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: Issue is clear. The root cause is well-identified — wrapper exits before bd state is flushed. Acceptance criteria are testable. One concern: 'settled post-submit state' is vague. What concrete signal should the wrapper wait for? Disappearance of the modal? Return to board? A specific text pattern? Why: the implementer needs a concrete condition, not just 'wait longer.' Suggested action: acceptance criteria should specify the observable signal (e.g., wait-for-no-text of the modal title, or wait-for-text of the board view). Verdict: EPIC BLOCKER — the wrapper's core value proposition is proving mutation worked; if it can't do that reliably, the toolkit is broken.

title: Wrapper mutation presets skip initial readiness wait
description:
On the seeded embedded fixture, verify_bwb_state_flow mutation presets can fail before detail opens because they send ENTER immediately after startup. Repro: create a fixture repo, then run the mutation-cancel preset without extra pre-steps. Actual: capture times out waiting for Detail because the preset fires before the initial board selection/readiness text is present. Expected: built-in mutation presets should wait for the initial board selection/readiness state themselves instead of requiring caller-supplied pre-steps.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Mutation-save and mutation-cancel presets explicitly wait for an initial board readiness signal before sending ENTER.
- [ ] The readiness signal is concrete and deterministic (for example `wait-for-text:Selected:` or equivalent current board-selection text), not a blind sleep.
- [ ] Seeded-fixture runs no longer require extra caller-supplied pre-steps to reach detail reliably.
acceptance_criteria: Mutation-save and mutation-cancel presets wait for initial board readiness/selection before sending ENTER; seeded-fixture runs no longer require extra pre-steps to reach detail reliably.
close_reason: Mutation presets now wait for deterministic board readiness (Selected:) before ENTER; seeded fixture mutation flows run reliably without pre-steps.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: Issue is well-scoped and clearly written. Acceptance criteria are testable. One suggestion: specify what 'board readiness' means concretely — e.g., wait for 'Selected: <issue>' pattern. Why: the fix implementer needs to know what text pattern the preset waits for, and 'readiness' is ambiguous. Suggested action: add the concrete wait pattern to acceptance criteria. Verdict: EPIC BLOCKER — without this fix, mutation presets are unusable without manual pre-steps, which defeats the purpose of presets.

title: Stale search golden files: TestRenderGoldens fails for results_with_preview_w120, results_narrow_w80, default_all_results_w120
description:
## Repro

```
go test ./internal/ui/search -run TestRenderGoldens -v
```

Three sub-tests fail (empty_results passes):
- results_with_preview_w120
- results_narrow_w80
- default_all_results_w120

Failure message: "output mismatch for <golden>.golden"

## Root cause (suspected)

Golden files are stale — likely after the metadata rail changes in beads-workbench-8haw or a related details/search layout change updated rendered output without regenerating goldens.

## Expected

All TestRenderGoldens sub-tests pass.

## Actual

Three out of four sub-tests fail with golden mismatch.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All TestRenderGoldens sub-tests in internal/ui/search pass.
- [ ] Goldens are regenerated or search rendering is corrected to match goldens.
close_reason: Duplicate of beads-workbench-aqps after triage: same internal/ui/search golden mismatch and failing cases.

title: Mutation catalog JSON decode blocks comment/create/update modals
description:
## Description
Fix mutation catalog loading so detail comment and board create and update modal flows open successfully on the seeded fixture.

## Instructions
1. Reproduce the seeded-fixture failure and inspect the catalog read path used by `loadMutationCatalogsCmd`.
2. Align the status, type, and label catalog gateway decoding and command usage with the actual `bd` JSON output.
3. Add or update focused gateway and app tests that cover successful catalog loading for mutation modal flows.
4. Verify seeded-fixture detail comment, board create, and board update actions open their modals without toast failure.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/gateway/beads/read_gateway.go`
- `internal/gateway/beads/read_payloads.go`
- `internal/gateway/beads/read_gateway_test.go`
- `internal/app/model.go`
- `internal/app/model_test.go`
acceptance_criteria: On the seeded fixture, detail comment, board create, and board update actions open their modals without a mutation-catalog decode toast.
notes: Investigating real bd label catalog JSON shape and patching gateway decode/tests for mutation modal flows.
close_reason: Fixed label catalog JSON decoding, added gateway + embedded-fixture regression coverage, and verified create/update/comment modals open without mutation-catalog toast.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Triage merged duplicate beads-workbench-lgih into this issue. This survivor now covers the same mutation-catalog JSON decode failure across detail comment and board create/update modal entry points on the seeded fixture.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Root cause confirmed: real `bd label list-all --json` returns objects like {"label":"area:app","count":2}, but the gateway decodes a []string. That decode failure aborts mutation catalog loading before create/update modals open.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Implemented local fix: decode `bd label list-all --json` as objects with a `label` field, updated gateway fixture shape, and added embedded-fixture coverage proving create/update/comment modals open without the mutation-catalog toast. Verified with focused gateway/app tests plus a built-binary capture run showing Create Issue, Update Issue bwf-2, and Comment on bwf-2 modals.

title: Wide details golden mismatch doubles dependency count in TestRenderWideThreeColumnGolden
description:
## Description
Fix `TestRenderWideThreeColumnGolden` so the wide details dependency header count reflects the intended rendered state.

## Instructions
1. Compare `dependencyGroups` and `countDependencyReferences` in `internal/ui/details/details.go` against the failing golden to determine whether the count or golden is wrong.
2. If the renderer is wrong, fix the dependency counting and grouping logic so the header count matches the intended unique dependency model.
3. If the renderer is correct, regenerate the focused golden and update the focused test expectation to match the intended behavior.
4. Keep the fix scoped to wide details rendering and add or adjust focused tests as needed.

## Files to Modify
- `internal/ui/details/details.go`
- `internal/ui/details/details_test.go`
- `internal/ui/details/testdata/wide_three_column.golden`
acceptance_criteria: Determine whether the wide details golden or renderer is wrong, then make go test ./internal/ui/details -run TestRenderWideThreeColumnGolden -v pass with the correct dependency count.
close_reason: No longer reproducible on current branch; likely fixed by abf5977 de-duplication in detail dependency groups.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Triage note: keep separate from the search golden failures. The key suspicious detail is the dependency count changing from 3 to 6 in ; treat this as a possible data/dedup or aggregation regression, not just a generic layout drift, until proven otherwise.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Correction to prior triage note: keep this separate from the search golden failures. The key suspicious detail is that TestRenderWideThreeColumnGolden changes the dependency count from 3 to 6; treat this as a possible data, aggregation, or dedup regression until proven to be only stale golden output.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Current main does not reproduce the triaged failure. `go test ./internal/ui/details -run TestRenderWideThreeColumnGolden -v` passes, `wide_three_column.golden` still expects a Dependencies header count of 3, and `countDependencyReferences` currently returns only blocked-by + blocks + related (not structure rows). This issue now looks stale/already fixed unless there is a missing repro outside the focused golden test.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Git history review: I could not find a committed regression where the Dependencies header changed from 3 to 6. `countDependencyReferences` was introduced in 6cfbd30 (`feat: rework issue detail dependencies layout and metadata actions`) and has always counted only blocked-by + blocks + related. The closest real fix is abf5977 (`fix: stabilize detail metadata interactions and verification tooling`), which added de-duplication in `dependencyGroups` so duplicate issue IDs do not render across visible groups. `dcc7873` introduced the original wide rail; `6cfbd30` added the Dependencies pane + Structure group; `abf5977` is the commit that most plausibly fixed the underlying duplication symptom. A literal header-count=6 regression does not appear in committed history, so the issue may have been filed from a transient local state or from interpreting Structure rows as dependency rows.

title: Acceptance Review: Strengthen real-app verification workflow with PTY flows, state checks, and debug evidence
description:
## Description
Run acceptance review for the small ad-hoc verification toolkit.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Design/spec task is closed.
- [ ] Implementation/helper task is closed.
- [ ] Documentation task is closed.
- [ ] The resulting toolkit is small, reliable enough for ad-hoc UI checks, and clearly not a broad framework.
- [ ] The final workflow covers visible UI evidence plus optional persisted-state verification for mutation flows.
- [ ] Any remaining gaps are captured as follow-up issues.
close_reason: All criteria verified

title: Implement at least one end-to-end runtime verification helper path for mutation flows
description:
## Description
Implement the minimal helper surface needed so the agent can run better ad-hoc real-app UI verification flows. Focus on practical scripts, not a general-purpose automation framework.

## Required Implementation Scope
### Priority order (MVP first)
1. `wait-for-text` / `wait-for-no-text` support in the PTY helper
2. optional checkpoints / intermediate snapshots
3. separate before/after state wrapper using `bd show --json`

### Extend `scripts/capture_bwb_screen.py`
Implement at least:
- send-key steps
- wait-for-text
- wait-for-no-text
- per-step timeout/error reporting
- wait-phase polling around 50ms
- optional checkpoints or intermediate screen snapshots
- structured JSON output that includes failure/checkpoint context
- guaranteed child cleanup on timeout, failure, crash, or interrupt paths

### Add one small wrapper script
Implement a separate tiny wrapper script that can:
- capture `bd show <id> --json` before
- run a PTY flow through the capture script
- capture `bd show <id> --json` after
- report field changes / no-change for a representative mutation flow

## Representative Flows To Support
- one read-only navigation flow
- one mutation save flow
- one mutation cancel/no-save flow

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The PTY helper supports condition-based multi-step ad-hoc flows.
- [ ] `wait-for-text` / `wait-for-no-text` are implemented as the MVP capability for reliable multi-step flows.
- [ ] Failures identify which step timed out or failed.
- [ ] Wait-phase polling is responsive enough for ad-hoc use (target around 50ms).
- [ ] The wrapper proves a mutation flow with before/after `bd show --json` evidence.
- [ ] Child cleanup is guaranteed on timeout, crash, interrupt, and normal exit paths.
- [ ] Cleanup behavior is validated explicitly enough that orphaned PTY child processes are not left behind.
- [ ] The implementation remains small and script/tooling oriented.
close_reason: Implemented PTY step DSL with wait-for-text/no-text + per-step failure context, checkpoint snapshots, structured JSON/cleanup reporting, and a separate before/after bd show wrapper with representative read-only/mutation save/mutation cancel flows.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Minimum viable capabilities — prioritize wait-for-text over checkpoints. What: The description mentions optionally add companion script for before/after bd show --json checks. That is useful but secondary. The primary value-add over the current script is wait-for-text between steps so multi-step flows are reliable. Why: Without wait-for-text, the tool is just the current script with more steps — still timing-dependent, still flaky. With wait-for-text alone, you get reliable multi-step flows even without state snapshots. Suggested action: Priority order should be (1) wait-for-text/wait-for-no-text in step syntax, (2) intermediate screen capture/checkpoint, (3) before/after state wrapper. Item 1 is the MVP. Items 2-3 are nice-to-have.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Missing AC — cleanup guarantee. What: No acceptance criterion requires that the PTY child process is always killed even if the script crashes or times out. Why: Orphaned bwb processes will confuse subsequent runs and potentially hold locks on the beads DB. The existing script has basic cleanup but extending it with wait loops increases the risk of edge cases. Suggested action: Add AC requiring that the child process is guaranteed killed on any exit path (timeout, crash, keyboard interrupt). Test this explicitly.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Pre-execution check: ticket appears stale vs comment decisions. Please fold reviewer guidance into Description/AC (MVP priority: wait-for-text/wait-for-no-text first; explicit AC for guaranteed child cleanup on timeout/crash/interrupt with validation). I’m pausing implementation to avoid guessing scope.

title: Document real-app verification workflow for UI flows with state assertions
description:
## Description
Document the ad-hoc runtime verification workflow in `docs/RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md` so it is concise, to the point, and usable later without rediscovery.

## Required Documentation Content
Keep this recipe-oriented and short. Include:
1. how to run a read-only navigation check with the PTY helper
2. how to run a mutation save check with PTY + before/after `bd show --json`
3. how to run a mutation cancel/no-save check
4. what common timeout/failure messages mean and how to interpret them

## Constraints
- Do not turn the doc into an architecture essay.
- Keep it practical enough that the agent can later use it directly during ad-hoc testing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The doc explains how to run the helper scripts for ad-hoc UI checks.
- [ ] It includes concise examples for navigation, save, and cancel verification.
- [ ] It explains timeout/failure interpretation briefly.
- [ ] It stays short and practical.
close_reason: Updated RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md with concise ad-hoc PTY recipes for navigation, save/cancel state assertions, and failure interpretation using existing capture_bwb_screen.py interface.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Doc scope guard — keep it to recipe format. What: AC says stays short and practical but does not define a length target. Why: Documentation tasks for tooling tend to balloon. The current RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md is 67 lines and already well-structured. Suggested action: Cap the additions at roughly the same size — add a new section with 3-4 copy-paste recipes (navigation check, mutation save check, mutation cancel check, interpreting failures). No architecture discussion, no rationale sections.

title: Design verification patterns for PTY flows, persisted-state checks, and optional debug hooks
description:
## Description
Define the concrete CLI/spec for the small ad-hoc verification toolkit. Keep this script-first and tightly scoped.

## Required Output of This Design Task
Produce a concise concrete design that defines:
1. exact PTY step syntax
2. exact PTY JSON output schema
3. exact per-step timeout behavior and failure reporting
4. polling behavior for wait phases (target around 50ms)
5. whether checkpoints are emitted inline or as named snapshots
6. the wrapper-script interface for before/after `bd show --json` checks
7. example commands for:
   - read-only navigation verification
   - mutation save verification
   - mutation cancel/no-save verification

## Constraints
- Keep the design small enough to implement directly in scripts.
- No framework-style branching or generalized test DSL.
- The design document/spec should stay short (roughly 30-50 lines or equivalent concise sections).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The PTY step syntax includes send-key and wait-for-text / wait-for-no-text behavior.
- [ ] The design includes per-step timeout semantics and step-specific error reporting.
- [ ] The design keeps the state-check wrapper separate from the PTY script.
- [ ] The design is specific enough that implementation can proceed without guessing.
close_reason: Delivered compact implementation-ready spec in issue comments covering PTY step syntax, JSON schema, timeout/polling/failure semantics, checkpoint model, separate state-wrapper interface, cleanup behavior, and three concrete example commands.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Scope risk — design task has no concrete output format defined. What: AC says choose a script-first approach and define minimal capabilities but does not say what the deliverable IS. A design task with vague deliverables tends to either produce a mini design doc nobody reads, or gets skipped because the implementer just starts coding. Why: Without a defined output, this task is hard to verify as done. Suggested action: Make the deliverable explicit — the design output should be the exact CLI interface (flags/step syntax), JSON output schema, and 2-3 example commands covering the target flows. That is the design. Nothing else needed.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Simplification — consider merging .1 and .2. What: The design task blocks implementation but the design is small enough (CLI interface + step syntax + output schema) that it can be a section at the top of the implementation task. Why: Separate design and implement tasks for a single-script extension adds coordination overhead without value. The implementer IS the designer here. Suggested action: Merge .1 into .2 as a design-then-implement task, or at minimum make .1 output just the CLI spec (30 lines max) so it closes fast.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Proposed compact spec for ad-hoc verification toolkit:

1) PTY step syntax (exact)
- CLI: verify-pty --cmd '<app command>' --step '<STEP>' [--step '<STEP>' ...] [--json-out <path>]
- STEP grammar (no branching):
  - send-key:<key>
  - wait-for-text:<literal text>[:<timeout-ms>]
  - wait-for-no-text:<literal text>[:<timeout-ms>]
  - checkpoint:<name>
- Key tokens: up,down,left,right,enter,esc,tab,shift+tab,ctrl+c,backspace,space,char:<c>
- Default timeout for wait steps: 2000ms (if omitted).

2) Polling + timeout behavior
- wait steps poll terminal buffer every 50ms.
- wait-for-text succeeds on first poll containing literal substring.
- wait-for-no-text succeeds on first poll where substring is absent.
- Timeout is per-step (not global): step fails when elapsed > timeout-ms.

3) Step failure reporting (JSON)
- Top-level JSON:
  {
    "ok": <bool>,
    "command": "...",
    "started_at": "RFC3339",
    "ended_at": "RFC3339",
    "steps": [ ... ],
    "checkpoints": [ ... ],
    "cleanup": {"status":"ok|forced","actions":["ctrl+c","term","kill"]}
  }
- Step entry schema:
  {
    "index": <int>,
    "raw": "wait-for-text:Saved:1500",
    "type": "send-key|wait-for-text|wait-for-no-text|checkpoint",
    "timeout_ms": <int|null>,
    "status": "pass|fail|skipped",
    "started_at": "RFC3339",
    "ended_at": "RFC3339",
    "error": {"code":"timeout|runtime","message":"..."}|null,
    "observed_excerpt": "last 20 lines joined"
  }

4) Checkpoints choice
- Use named snapshots (checkpoint:<name>) stored in top-level checkpoints array; no inline blob on every step.
- checkpoint entry: {"name":"...","step_index":<int>,"captured_at":"RFC3339","screen_excerpt":"last 40 lines"}.

5) Cleanup expectations on timeout/failure
- On first step failure: stop executing remaining steps (mark remaining as skipped), then cleanup child process:
  1) send ctrl+c, wait 300ms
  2) if alive send SIGTERM, wait 700ms
  3) if alive send SIGKILL
- Record performed cleanup actions in cleanup.actions.

6) Separate wrapper for persisted-state checks
- Wrapper CLI (separate script): verify-state-wrap --issue <id> --pty-cmd '<verify-pty ...>' --expect-change '<jq expr on {before,after}>'
- Wrapper flow:
  1) before=
  2) run verify-pty
  3) after=
  4) evaluate jq expression against {before,after}
- Wrapper exit fails if PTY fails OR jq expectation is false.

7) Example commands
- Read-only navigation:
  verify-pty --cmd 'beads-workbench' --step 'wait-for-text:Ready:3000' --step 'send-key:down' --step 'send-key:enter' --step 'wait-for-text:Issue Details:1500' --step 'checkpoint:details-open' --step 'send-key:esc' --step 'wait-for-no-text:Issue Details:1500'
- Mutation save verification:
  verify-state-wrap --issue beads-workbench-ay5j --pty-cmd "verify-pty --cmd 'beads-workbench' --step 'send-key:e' --step 'wait-for-text:Edit:1500' --step 'send-key:char:x' --step 'send-key:ctrl+s' --step 'wait-for-text:Saved:1500'" --expect-change '.before.title != .after.title'
- Mutation cancel/no-save verification:
  verify-state-wrap --issue beads-workbench-ay5j --pty-cmd "verify-pty --cmd 'beads-workbench' --step 'send-key:e' --step 'wait-for-text:Edit:1500' --step 'send-key:char:x' --step 'send-key:esc' --step 'wait-for-no-text:Edit:1500'" --expect-change '.before == .after'

This keeps the toolkit script-first, minimal, and implementation-ready without a generalized DSL.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Correction: wrapper flow step text in prior comment should read literally: (1) before=$(bd show <id> --json), (2) run verify-pty, (3) after=$(bd show <id> --json), (4) evaluate jq on {before,after}. No command substitution is intended in the tracker comment itself.

title: Strengthen real-app verification workflow with PTY flows, state checks, and debug evidence
description:
## Description
Build a small ad-hoc verification toolkit that helps the agent/operator test the real UI properly during manual/runtime review. This is **not** a generic automated UI test framework. The toolkit should be centered on the existing PTY capture script plus a tiny persisted-state wrapper script.

## Intent / Non-Goals
### Goal
- help the agent run better real-app checks during implementation and review
- support flows like: open detail, move to Dependencies / Metadata, press up/down/enter/escape, inspect the resulting UI, and optionally compare `bd show --json` before/after

### Non-goal
- do not build a large new automated UI framework
- do not add branching flow logic, retries-as-a-framework, or general orchestration abstractions
- do not replace deterministic unit/integration tests

## Reviewer-Guided Reliability Requirements
- Step execution must be condition-based for multi-step flows, not only blind sleeps.
- The PTY helper must support waiting for text to appear and disappear.
- Waiting must use explicit per-step timeouts with clear failure messages identifying the step that hung.
- Polling during wait phases should be frequent enough to stay responsive (target around 50ms, not coarse 250ms waits).
- Cleanup must remain guaranteed on timeout/failure.

## Proposed Shape
### Script 1 — extend `scripts/capture_bwb_screen.py`
Add only the minimum reliable ad-hoc capabilities:
- send-key steps
- wait-for-text steps
- wait-for-no-text steps
- optional checkpoints / intermediate screen captures
- per-step timeout reporting
- structured JSON output with step/checkpoint context

### Script 2 — add a tiny wrapper (separate script)
Purpose:
- snapshot `bd show --json` before a mutation flow
- run a PTY flow via the capture script
- snapshot `bd show --json` after the flow
- report changed/unchanged state for selected fields

Keep this wrapper very small and recipe-oriented.

## Scope Boundary
If a flow can be described as:
- send key
- wait for text / wait for no text
- optionally checkpoint
then it is in scope.

If it requires general branching logic, retries as a framework feature, or broad orchestration semantics, it is out of scope.

## Concrete Design Deliverables
The design/implementation should specify:
- exact PTY step syntax
- exact PTY JSON output shape
- exact wrapper usage pattern for before/after `bd show --json`
- example command for verifying status save
- example command for verifying cancel-does-not-save

## Success Criteria
- [ ] A small helper script/toolkit exists for ad-hoc real-app UI checks.
- [ ] The PTY helper supports condition-based multi-step flows reliably enough for ad-hoc use.
- [ ] A tiny wrapper supports before/after `bd show --json` verification for mutation flows.
- [ ] `docs/RUNTIME_UI_VERIFICATION.md` documents the workflow concisely with practical recipes.
close_reason: Verified via ay5j.4: epic acceptance criteria met

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Overall verdict: The plan direction is sound — extending the existing PTY script rather than building a framework is correct. But the current capture script has a reliability hole that will bite you in multi-step flows, and the task breakdown front-loads design without enough constraint on what design produces. See per-task comments for specifics.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Reliability concern — settle_wait break logic is fragile for multi-step flows. What: The current capture loop (line 108-122) breaks after settle_wait seconds of silence ONLY after all steps are sent. But for multi-step flows, there is no mechanism to confirm the UI actually processed a keystroke before sending the next one. A 0.2s delay between steps is just a guess — if the app is slow to render, the next key hits the wrong screen. Why: This is the number one thing that will make the tool unreliable. Blind timing between steps means flaky results on slow machines or under load. Suggested action: The extended script needs wait-for-text between steps, not just fixed delays. Steps should be expressible as send KEY then wait until PATTERN appears rather than send KEY after Ns.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Reliability concern — no per-step timeout. What: The current timeout is global only. If wait-for-text is added, each wait needs its own timeout so you get a clear error like timed out waiting for text X after step 3 instead of generic capture timed out after 10s. Why: Debugging failures without knowing WHICH step hung is painful. Suggested action: Each wait-for-text step should have a per-step timeout defaulting to ~5s, global timeout remains as safety net.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Reliability concern — polling frequency. What: When adding wait-for-text, the polling loop needs ~50ms select timeout to check the pyte screen frequently. The current settle_wait of 0.25s is fine for final capture but too slow for condition polling. Why: Slow polling means slow tests. Suggested action: Use ~50ms select timeout during wait-for-text phases, only use longer settle_wait for final capture.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text:
Epic verification: NOT VERIFIED/CLOSED.
Acceptance review found wrapper-flow defects (ypab, lsbv, g2jw) and exploratory create/update modal failure (lgih). Unrelated repository test failure also tracked in aqps.
Next: resolve blockers, rerun ay5j.4, then re-check epic closure.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review of blocker set: 4 of 6 reported bugs are epic blockers (ypab, lsbv, g2jw, wd3a). They cover wrapper preset reliability and doc alignment — both are core epic deliverables. 2 bugs (lgih, aqps) are unrelated and should be tracked separately. The epic cannot close until all 4 blockers are resolved and ay5j.4 is re-verified. Note: task .3 (docs) was closed prematurely given wd3a — consider whether .3 needs reopening or whether wd3a alone is sufficient to track the gap.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Acceptance review rerun passed via beads-workbench-ay5j.4. Toolkit stays small/script-oriented (static steps, waits, checkpoints, before/after wrapper) without framework branching/retries. Separate follow-up bugs remain tracked: aqps, lgih, kq87.

title: Document auto-refresh behavior in user-facing help/docs
description: Discovered during acceptance review of beads-workbench-t7o.4. Expected: user-facing help/docs reflect the new auto-refresh behavior because reload expectations changed (focus-regain refresh and low-frequency scheduled refresh now exist alongside manual r reload). Actual: current help/docs only describe manual reload keys; grep across docs/*.md and docs/user-guide/*.md found no user-facing mention of auto-refresh behavior, cadence, or how it relates to manual reload. This leaves operators without guidance on why views may refresh on focus regain / on a timer or when manual reload is still needed.
acceptance_criteria:
- User-facing help text or docs mention focus-regain and scheduled auto-refresh behavior
- Manual reload key descriptions remain clear and are not misleading after the auto-refresh change
- Acceptance review criterion for documented behavior expectations is satisfied
close_reason: Added concise user-facing auto-refresh guidance and clarified manual reload key descriptions in key bindings docs.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Execution started from acceptance-review follow-up. Goal: add user-facing auto-refresh guidance without making manual reload bindings misleading.

title: dependency-only detail view shows duplicate Structure section
description:
Discovered during verification of beads-workbench-2l4 in the built binary against the embedded fixture repo. Expected: when an issue has blocked/blocks/related refs but no parent-group structure, the Dependencies pane shows Blocked by / Blocks / Related rows only, with selection moving across those rows. Actual: after browser-item population falls back to dependency refs, the detail renderer also shows a Structure section containing the same refs even though no parent-group context exists.

## Root Cause
`dependencyGroups()` appends a Structure section unconditionally from `browserItems`. After the model populates `browserItems` from dependency fallback rather than parent-group context, those same refs appear twice.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] When BrowserItems are populated from dependency refs because no parent-group exists, the Structure section does not appear in Dependencies.
- [ ] When real parent-group structure exists, the Structure section still appears as intended.
- [ ] Real-app review confirms dependency-only detail view no longer duplicates dependency rows.
close_reason: Fixed dependency fallback rendering to suppress Structure group unless parent-group context exists; added regression tests for fallback and parent-group paths; focused detail/app tests pass.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Root cause confirmed at details.go:270-271. What: dependencyGroups() unconditionally appends a Structure section from browserItems. After 2l4 populated browserItems from BlockedBy+Blocks+Related refs when no parent-group exists, these same refs now appear in both their typed sections (Blocked by/Blocks/Related) AND the Structure section. Why: Structure is meant for parent-group browsing context (parent + siblings), not a flat dump of dependency refs. When browserItems come from dependencies, Structure just duplicates them. Suggested action: suppress the Structure group when browserItems were populated from dependencies rather than from a parent-group. The simplest approach: add a flag (e.g., BrowserFromParentGroup bool) to the detail state, and only render Structure when that flag is true.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Missing acceptance criterion. What: no criterion says Structure must not render when browserItems come from dependency fallback. Why: a tasker could suppress duplicate rows in some other way (deduplication) rather than fixing the actual semantic problem — Structure has no meaning without parent-group context. Suggested action: add criterion: 'When BrowserItems are populated from dependency refs (no parent-group), the Structure section must not appear in the Dependencies pane.'

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Ordering relative to 74r/6eq. What: yxjx touches dependencyGroups() in details.go which affects rendered row count in the Dependencies pane. 74r changes pane width. Why: fixing yxjx removes the entire Structure section for dependency-only issues, reducing pane content. Width tuning in 74r should account for the final content, not the inflated duplicate. Suggested action: fix yxjx before 74r. yxjx and 6sk are independent and can run in parallel.

title: Related links render under Blocked by/Blocks instead of Related
description:
Discovered during verification of beads-workbench-59z while augmenting the embedded fixture with a `bd dep relate` link. Expected: a relates-to link created with `bd dep relate <id1> <id2>` appears in detail-mode under the Related group. Actual: `bd show --json` returns the link with dependency_type `relates-to`, but the gateway/detail pipeline only treats dependency_type `related` as related work, so the app renders the linked issue under Blocked by/Blocks and leaves Related empty.

## Root Cause
Gateway normalization only recognizes `related` and misses `relates-to`, even though `bd dep relate` emits `relates-to`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Gateway normalization classifies both `related` and `relates-to` dependency types as related work.
- [ ] Unit tests cover both dependency type strings.
- [ ] Real-app detail view shows `bd dep relate` links under Related rather than Blocked by/Blocks.
close_reason: Fixed dependency-type normalization for related/relates-to and added unit coverage for both variants in gateway detail mapping.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: Root cause confirmed — single-line fix. What: read_gateway.go:543 checks dependencyType == "related" but bd dep relate produces dependency_type "relates-to". The condition needs to also match "relates-to". Why: this is a data contract mismatch between bd CLI output and gateway parsing. Every relates-to link falls through to blockedBy (line 557). Suggested action: change line 543 to 'if dependencyType == "related" || dependencyType == "relates-to"'. Add a test case with dependency_type "relates-to" in read_gateway_test.go.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: Bug is well-scoped and actionable. One minor wording improvement. What: the repro steps say 'run bd create --id bwf-6 ... and bd dep relate bwf-2 bwf-6' but the acceptance criteria don't explicitly state the gateway must handle both 'related' and 'relates-to' dependency types. Why: a tasker might fix only 'relates-to' and break the existing 'related' path, or vice versa. Suggested action: add acceptance criterion: 'dependencyReferencesFromPayload classifies both dependency_type "related" and "relates-to" as related work, verified by unit tests for each.'

title: Content pane scroll continues or feels wrong after repeated down-key scrolling
description:
Discovered from user review after epic beads-workbench-90w was closed. Expected: content scrolling in detail mode should move predictably with each key press and stop immediately when key input stops. Actual: user reports that when content exceeds the page and they press Down, scrolling feels slow and appears to continue after they stop pressing.

## Investigation First
Code inspection suggests detail scrolling is currently discrete integer-offset scrolling with no animation, so this may be terminal key-repeat event queuing rather than a renderer bug. Verify that hypothesis before changing code.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Reproduce with controlled single-tap vs key-hold behavior and determine whether extra motion is caused by queued repeat key events, render lag, or a detail-mode state bug.
- [ ] If single discrete taps already behave correctly, capture the finding and decide whether this should be documented or handled differently.
- [ ] If a real detail-mode bug exists, file or convert this issue into a concrete implementation fix with exact reproduction and expected behavior.
close_reason: Verified: single-tap scroll is correct; apparent continued motion comes from queued repeated Down key events, not a detail-mode bug

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review — scroll architecture analysis: The scroll implementation is purely integer-offset based (applyScrollAction at model.go:370-386) with move=1 per key press. There is NO animation, momentum, or async scroll. The 'continues after key stops' symptom is almost certainly terminal key-repeat buffering: held keys generate a burst of KeyMsg events that queue in Bubble Tea's event loop, and the UI processes them after the key is released. This is not a bug in this codebase — it's standard terminal key-repeat behavior. What: the reported symptom is likely terminal key-repeat event queuing, not a scroll state bug. The 'slow' feel is because move=1 scrolls one line per keypress, which at terminal repeat rates feels sluggish for long content. Why: if the real problem is key-repeat perception, the fix is either (a) debouncing scroll events or (b) increasing scroll step size, NOT fixing scroll state logic. Suggested action: (1) verify by testing with a single discrete key tap — if one tap = one line and it stops, it's key-repeat. (2) If confirmed, downgrade to P2 and consider increasing scroll-down step to 2-3 lines, or adding j/k acceleration on held keys. Do NOT redesign the scroll system for a non-bug.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Set review — investigation-first framing is correct, likely a non-bug. What: the scroll code (applyScrollAction, move=1) is purely discrete with no animation or momentum. The prior reviewer's analysis is accurate — this is almost certainly terminal key-repeat buffering. The acceptance criteria correctly require reproduction before any code change. Why: this should not block any other work in the set. If investigation confirms key-repeat, the only reasonable action is either (a) close as wontfix/not-a-bug, or (b) optionally increase scroll step to 2-3 lines for faster traversal. Suggested action: deprioritize to last in execution order. Have the implementer do a single-tap test; if single tap = exactly one line of scroll, close this issue with a finding comment. Do not redesign scroll.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier: reproduced with embedded fixture + PTY capture. Single Down produced one discrete content-step; rapid repeated Down produced matching discrete queued steps that rendered shortly after the last key. No async momentum or detail-mode state bug found. Artifact: /tmp/bwb-verify-pxs-t8fTvk

title: Metadata pane should support up/down field selection and enter-based editing beyond status
description:
Discovered from user review after epic beads-workbench-90w was closed. Expected: the Metadata pane should support actionable field navigation with up/down and Enter for a constrained set of editable fields in detail mode. Actual: current behavior only meaningfully supports status and does not expose a true field-by-field navigation/editing model. This is follow-up product work rather than a regression bug.

## Scope Constraint
Limit this follow-up to a small v1 metadata interaction surface:
- editable fields: `Status` and `Priority` only
- non-editable informational rows remain read-only

## Interaction Model
- Up/down moves selection between editable metadata fields only.
- Enter on `Status` opens the existing status dialog.
- Enter on `Priority` cycles through priorities in place (`P0 -> P1 -> P2 -> P3 -> P4 -> P0`).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Metadata field navigation is explicitly limited to Status and Priority for this follow-up.
- [ ] Up/down navigates between Status and Priority only.
- [ ] Enter on Status triggers the existing status edit flow.
- [ ] Enter on Priority cycles priority through P0..P4 and wraps back to P0.
- [ ] Non-editable informational rows remain readable but are not treated as actionable fields.
close_reason: Implemented v1 metadata interaction model limited to Status/Priority; added navigation/edit behavior and focused tests.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Review: This is a feature request, not a bug — current behavior is as designed (status-only editing). Reclassify as type:task or type:enhancement. Hidden complexity: adding field selection requires (1) defining which metadata fields are editable vs read-only, (2) an edit interaction model per field type (priority is numeric, owner is text/selector, labels are multi-value), (3) rendering selected-field highlighting in metadata pane. What: the description says 'up/down field selection and Enter-based editing' but doesn't specify which fields or what the edit UX looks like for non-status fields. Why: without scoping which fields are editable and how, this will balloon into an open-ended design task. Suggested action: constrain to status + priority only as v1, with explicit edit interaction for each (status=dialog, priority=cycle or dialog). Defer labels/owner editing.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Set review — scope constraint is good but edit UX still undefined. What: the description says 'enter triggers the correct edit action' and scope constraint says 'status and priority only', but it doesn't specify what the edit interaction IS for priority. Status already has a dialog — what does priority editing look like? Cycle through P0-P5? A dialog? Inline text entry? Why: without specifying the interaction model, this will stall during implementation. The field navigation part (up/down between status and priority) is trivial; the edit UX is where the real work lives. Suggested action: specify 'priority edit = cycle P0→P1→P2→P3→P4→P5→P0 on Enter' or similar concrete interaction. Also: the existing metadataField struct already has an editable bool — add editable:true to Priority in metadataGroups and add MetadataFieldPriority constant. This is the only new feature in the set; defer it to last.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Verifier rerun: PASS. Focused tests passed; live embedded-fixture run confirmed metadata selection only toggles Status/Priority, Enter on Status opens Update Status modal, and Enter on Priority updates bwf-2 from P0 to P1. Follow-up bug filed for capture harness timeout on open modal: beads-workbench-nh66.

title: Add focus-aware and scheduled auto-refresh
description:
## Description
Implement shell-managed auto-refresh so the visible workbench view stays current without manual reload. Combine focus-regain refresh, scheduled stale refresh, and existing mutation-driven refresh while keeping board/search/detail UX stable.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Focus regain refreshes the active view so returning users see current data
- [ ] Active-view auto-refresh runs on a low-frequency schedule (target about 60s), not aggressive polling
- [ ] Existing mutation-triggered detail refresh behavior remains intact
- [ ] Writes that invalidate browse data mark board/search dirty for the next eligible active-view refresh
- [ ] Board/search/detail interactions remain stable during auto-refresh; disruptive regressions are tracked or fixed
- [ ] Acceptance review task passes before epic closure
close_reason: Completed focus-aware and scheduled auto-refresh: shell focus/tick orchestration, safe board/search refresh, dirty/stale active-surface policy, follow-up fixes, and acceptance review all passed.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Plan review: dependency structure is correct. t7o.1 and t7o.2 are independent and parallelizable, t7o.3 integrates both, t7o.4 gates closure. The 3-task + acceptance breakdown is right-sized for this scope — no need to split or merge.

comments.author: Hans Kohlreiter
comments.text: Risk: epic success criteria bullet 3 says 'existing mutation-triggered refresh behavior remains intact and is integrated with dirty/stale tracking.' This conflates a regression guard (keep existing behavior) with new feature work (dirty/stale integration). Why: the tasker may treat 'integrated' as mandatory scope creep vs a nice-to-have.
